prompt: 2026 USER EMPATHY on the LEAD TIME of security audits / reviews / pentests. A software company (often shipping AI) must pass a security audit or review to close an enterprise deal. The traditional path has lead time: schedule an external audit/pentest firm, wait weeks or months, expensive, lots of back-and-forth, and you get a static PDF the buyer has to take on trust. Find real 2026 posts/threads (@handle/source + date, verbatim) where founders/engineers/security people complain about: how LONG a security audit or pentest takes, scheduling/waitlists, the cost, the lead time blocking a deal, wanting it faster / on-demand / continuous / self-serve. How do they describe the wait and the pain in their own words? Does an ON-DEMAND, instant, repeatable security audit (vs a weeks-long engagement) resonate as a strong value prop? List the 10 most resonant verbatim phrases about audit/pentest lead time and the desire for something faster.
content:
**Yes, an on-demand/instant/repeatable/continuous/self-serve security audit resonates strongly as a value proposition.** Multiple 2026 posts explicitly contrast traditional weeks/months-long, expensive, static PDF engagements (which become outdated quickly, especially for fast-shipping AI/software teams) with the desire or reality of continuous, agentic/AI-driven, on-demand testing that keeps pace with deployments and changes. Complaints center on scheduling delays, high costs ($30k–$85k+), superficial or ignored reports, and deals/velocity blocked by slow processes—directly validating the pain points described.[[1]](https://x.com/_avichawla/status/2043958083832197470)[[1]](https://x.com/_avichawla/status/2043958083832197470)

Here are real, verbatim examples from X posts (with @handle + date). I prioritized the most directly relevant 2026 complaints about lead time, wait/scheduling, cost, blocking deals/velocity, and the explicit desire for faster/continuous/on-demand alternatives:

- **@_avichawla, Tue, 14 Apr 2026**: “A typical pentest today costs $30k-$50k per engagement, takes 4-6 weeks of scoping, and produces a PDF that's outdated the moment a PR merges. That worked when teams shipped slowly. But it falls apart when you're deploying multiple times a day...” (advocates continuous AI pentesting that “run[s] at the pace devs actually ship... before releases, after major changes, and continuously”).[[1]](https://x.com/_avichawla/status/2043958083832197470)

- **@Akitra_Inc, Tue, 09 Jun 2026**: “You ran a pen test. Fixed the findings. Now you're waiting. Your environment didn't wait with you. Continuous > Periodic. Always.”[[2]](https://x.com/Akitra_Inc/status/2064173133905719728)

- **@polsia, Sun, 07 Jun 2026**: “Your pentest passed 3 months ago. Your threat landscape? Completely different now. Pentests are snapshots. TTPs change weekly. By implementation, attackers moved on. Continuous adversary simulation catches what annual pentests miss.”[[3]](https://x.com/polsia/status/2063591569413079223)

- **@jayson, Sat, 06 Jun 2026**: “The approved enterprise AI tool: 8 weeks of security review. The shadow IT version everyone actually uses: installed during the security review.”[[4]](https://x.com/jayson/status/2063320515260060138)

- **@cyber_razz, Mon, 30 Mar 2026**: “We hired a pentesting firm... They spent six hours running automated tools we already owned. Then they sent us a 147-page PDF with red text everywhere. They charged us $85K for this privilege... The grand conclusion was that we should enforce MFA and update our patches. I'd been saying both for years.”[[5]](https://x.com/cyber_razz/status/2038659676951224519)

- **@gabbytech01, Wed, 03 Jun 2026**: “A company paid for a pentest. I sent the report 14 critical vulnerabilities. Three months later they called again. ‘We got breached.’ I opened the report. The breach came through vulnerability number 3. The report was read. But nothing was fixed. Security is not a document.”[[6]](https://x.com/gabbytech01/status/2062298933791977708)

- **@sooyoon_eth, Tue, 02 Jun 2026** (in thread on agentic testing): “this is the real bottleneck. if vuln discovery cost doesn't drop, teams will default to the yearly pentest. continuous agentic testing (acost) changes the math because you validate changes on every deploy.”[[7]](https://x.com/sooyoon_eth/status/2061919078982566287)

- **@DbsCrypto, Mon, 08 Jun 2026**: “Shared tables are cheap until the first security review. Then ‘customer_id filtering’ stops sounding like architecture and starts sounding like a liability. The deal doesn’t die on scale. It dies on proof.” (highlighting security review as deal blocker).[[8]](https://x.com/DbsCrypto/status/2064117771290415434)

- **@commando_skiipz, Tue, 31 Mar 2026** (context on rushed/superficial vendor pentests for banks): Highlights industry issues with scanner-based “pentests” that fail real scrutiny, implying inadequate depth/speed/quality in traditional processes.[[9]](https://x.com/commando_skiipz/status/2039035466897301507)

- **@aesirxio, Wed, 03 Jun 2026**: “Compliance documents don't fail at the policy review. They fail when a regulator asks for proof, and the organization needs three weeks to assemble spreadsheets. Audit readiness is an operating condition, not a document.” (emphasizing slow, manual audit readiness).[[10]](https://x.com/aesirxio/status/2062058344479855080)

These phrases capture the core frustrations with traditional lead times (4–8+ weeks or months for scheduling/scoping/reporting), costs, static/outdated outputs, and resulting deal/operational friction—while repeatedly calling for or praising continuous, faster, or automated alternatives. On-demand/instant options would directly address the “waiting,” “snapshot,” “outdated the moment a PR merges,” and “8 weeks of security review” pain points.
